samples:
BG 2.47 — verse well-known, certain.

Text: *"karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana / mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi."* Meaning: right to action only, never to fruits; don't act for reward, don't cling to inaction.

Principle: **act fully, detach from outcome.** Do duty well, release grip on result.
BG 2.47: "You have right to action, not to fruits of action. Let not fruit be your motive, nor cling to inaction." Certain of this verse — famous line, "karmanye vadhikaraste ma phaleshu kadachana."

Principle: **do your duty fully, detach from outcome.** Act well, drop obsession over reward.
BG 2.47 — famous verse. Sanskrit: *"karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana..."* Says: you have right to action alone, never to its fruits; let not fruit-of-action be your motive, nor cling to inaction. Single principle: **do your duty fully, but detach from the results** — act without attachment to outcome. Confident of this verse.

BG 2.48: "Yogasthah kuru karmani, sangam tyaktva dhananjaya / siddhy-asiddhyoh samo bhutva, samatvam yoga uchyate." Krishna tell Arjuna — do your duty established in yoga, drop attachment, stay even in success and failure; that evenness is yoga. Confident on this one.

Principle: **act fully, detach from outcome.** Control effort, not result. Equanimity toward win/loss.
BG 2.48 — Krishna to Arjuna: *"yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi... samatvaṁ yoga ucyate"* — "Established in yoga, do your duty, drop attachment, stay equal in success and failure; that evenness is yoga." Certain of this verse. Principle: **act without clinging to outcome** — full effort in the deed, equanimity toward result. (Sets up famous 2.47 idea: right to action, not to fruits.)
BG 2.48 — confident. Krishna: "yogasthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā dhanañjaya, siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṁ yoga ucyate." Meaning: established in yoga, do your duty, drop attachment, stay equal in success and failure — this evenness of mind is called yoga. Single principle: **act fully, but detach from outcome** — commit to right action, stay equanimous whether it succeeds or fails.

BG 2.62 (fairly certain):

*"dhyāyato viṣayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate / saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate"* — Dwelling on sense-objects breeds attachment; attachment breeds desire; desire breeds anger. Verse maps causal chain of downfall (next verse continues: anger→delusion→lost memory→ruined intellect→destruction). Single principle: **guard attention at root** — control what mind dwells on early, before chain runs, because each link auto-triggers next.
BG 2.62 — confident.

Verse: *dhyāyato viṣayān puṃsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate, saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ, kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate* — "Dwelling on sense objects breeds attachment; from attachment springs desire; from desire, anger." Maps the chain of downfall (continues in 2.63: anger → delusion → lost memory → ruined intellect). Principle it encodes: **guard your attention** — what mind repeatedly dwells on becomes craving, so control ruin at the root by not feeding the mind's fixation on sense objects.
BG 2.62 — "dhyāyato viṣayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate / saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate": man who dwells on sense objects breeds attachment to them; attachment breeds desire; thwarted desire breeds anger. Start of causal ladder (2.63 continues: anger → delusion → lost memory → wrecked reason → ruin). Principle it encodes: guard attention at first link — brooding on what you crave is the seed, so control the mind's dwelling early, not the anger late. Confident on this verse.

BG 3.35: "Better own dharma done imperfectly than another's dharma done well. Better to die in own duty; another's path breeds danger/fear." (*śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ...*) — Krishna to Arjuna. Confident on this one.

Principle: **act from your own nature/role, not by imitating someone else's** — authenticity over borrowed excellence.
BG 3.35 — confident. Verse: "śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt; sva-dharme nidhanaṁ śreyaḥ para-dharmo bhayāvahaḥ." Meaning: better own dharma (duty/nature) done imperfectly than another's done well; better to die in own dharma, another's brings danger. Principle: **follow your own path/duty, flaws and all — don't imitate someone else's life just because it looks better.** Authenticity over imitation.
BG 3.35: "Better own duty (svadharma) done imperfect than another's duty done well. Death in own duty better; another's path brings fear/danger." Sanskrit: *śreyān svadharmo viguṇaḥ paradharmāt svanuṣṭhitāt...*

Principle: **follow your own nature/role, not imitate another's** — authenticity beats borrowed perfection.

Confidence high on this verse. (Nearly same idea repeats BG 18.47.)

BG 4.18: "One who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction — that one is wise among humans, a yogi, doer of all deeds" (*karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed...*). Fairly certain of this verse. Principle: act fully but stay inwardly unattached to results — outer action, inner stillness. Do the work; drop the grasping for its fruit.
BG 4.18: "karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ / sa buddhimān manuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsna-karma-kṛt" — one who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is wise among men, a yogi, doer of all deeds. Reasonably certain of this verse. Principle: act fully but stay inwardly detached from results — outer doing, inner stillness.
BG 4.18 (fairly confident): "One who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction — that one is wise among men, a yogi, doer of all action." Sanskrit: *karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ...* Principle: act fully but stay inwardly unattached to results — do work without ego-bondage to it. Right action needs no anxious grasping; wisdom sees stillness inside activity.

BG 6.16 — Chapter 6 (Dhyāna Yoga). Sanskrit opens *"nātyaśnatas tu yogo 'sti na caikāntam anaśnataḥ..."* Meaning: yoga not for one who eats too much, nor one who eats too little; not for one who sleeps too much, nor stays awake too much. Pairs with 6.17 (the positive: moderate eating, sleep, work → yoga destroys sorrow).

Principle: **moderation**. Extremes — indulgence or self-denial — both block the disciplined mind; balance is the ground for practice.

Confidence high on this verse.
BG 6.16: yoga not for one who eats too much, nor one who eats too little; not for one who sleeps too much, nor one who stays awake too much. Principle: **moderation** — mastery need balanced habits, not extremes. Reasonably certain of this verse; next verse (6.17) extend same idea to work and recreation.
BG 6.16 — "nātyaśnatas tu yogo 'sti na caikāntam anaśnataḥ / na cāti-svapna-śīlasya jāgrato naiva cārjuna." Krishna say: yoga not for one who eat too much or eat nothing, nor one who sleep too much or stay awake too much. Principle: **moderation** — balance in eating, sleeping, activity. Extremes block the path; middle way enable it. (6.17 finish thought: measured habits kill sorrow.) Confident on this verse.

BG 2.50 — "buddhi-yukto jahatiha ubhe sukrita-duskrite / tasmad yogaya yujyasva yogah karmasu kausalam." One with evenness of mind sheds both good and bad deeds here in this life; so yoke to yoga — **yoga is skill in action** (yogah karmasu kausalam). Principle: act with equanimity, detached from outcome. Do work skillfully, drop attachment to result. Fairly confident on verse.
BG 2.50 — "buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte, tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam." Krishna say: one with even mind (buddhi-yoga) drop both good and bad karma here in this life. So yoke to yoga. Famous last line — *yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam*, "yoga is skill in action."

Principle: act with skill and equanimity, unattached to fruit. Do work well, let go of outcome.

Certain of verse.
BG 2.50 — confident.

Verse: *"buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte / tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam."* One steady in wisdom/equanimity sheds both good and bad deeds even in this life; so yoke to yoga — **yoga is skill in action** (*yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam*). Principle it encodes: act with full skill and effort, but detach from clinging to results — equanimity of mind *is* the skill, not the outcome.

BG 18.16 — fairly confident, not certain on exact wording.

Verse: *"tatraivaṁ sati kartāram ātmānaṁ kevalaṁ tu yaḥ / paśyaty akṛtabuddhitvān na sa paśyati durmatiḥ."* Sits in Krishna's teaching on five factors of action (seat, doer, instruments, effort, divine/daiva). Says: one who — from untrained intellect — sees pure self alone as sole doer does not truly see; such mind flawed. Practical principle it encodes: **drop the ego of sole doership.** Action come from many causes, not you alone. Act, but no claim "I alone did it" — kills pride and delusion both.
BG 18.16 — reasonably sure of this one.

Verse follows 18.13–15, which list five factors of any action (body/seat, the doer, the instruments/senses, the various efforts, and daivam — providence/fate). 18.16 says: one who sees **the self alone** as the doer, ignoring those five factors, has impure understanding and does not see truly.

**Principle: kill the sole-doer ego.** No action springs from you alone — many causes converge. Claiming full credit = flawed sight. Act, but drop the "I alone did this."

Caveat: wording of translations vary; gist above solid, exact phrasing not.
BG 18.16 — reasonably certain. Text: *"tatraivaṁ sati kartāram ātmānaṁ kevalaṁ tu yaḥ / paśyaty akṛta-buddhitvān na sa paśyati durmatiḥ."* Follows 18.14's list of five factors behind any action (body, the doer, the senses, effort, and daiva/divine will); Krishna says whoever — from untrained intellect — sees the pure self *alone* as the sole doer sees wrongly, is of poor understanding. Practical principle: **agency is distributed, not solo** — drop the ego-claim of "I alone did this," which cuts both pride in success and crushing guilt in failure.

BG 18.63 — Krishna to Arjuna: "iti te jñānam ākhyātaṁ guhyād guhyataraṁ mayā / vimṛśyaitad aśeṣeṇa yathecchasi tathā kuru" — "Thus I told you knowledge, secret of secrets. Reflect on it fully, then do as you wish." Reasonably certain. Principle: teacher gives full knowledge, then hands back agency. Reflect deeply, then choose freely — no coercion. Informed autonomy.
BG 18.63 — Krishna's closing line to Arjuna: *"iti te jñānam ākhyātaṁ guhyād guhyataraṁ mayā / vimṛśyaitad aśeṣeṇa yathecchasi tathā kuru"* — "Thus I have declared to you knowledge more secret than all secrets. Reflect on it fully, then do as you wish." Principle: **deliberate freely, then choose — autonomy after reflection.** Krishna gives the teaching complete but leaves the decision to Arjuna; no coercion. Reasonably confident on verse and gist.
Confident on this verse.

**BG 18.63** — Krishna to Arjuna: "Thus I have declared to you knowledge more secret than all secrets. Reflect on it fully, then do as you wish" (*yathecchasi tatha kuru*).

**Principle:** counsel given, choice left free. Deliberate fully — then act by own will. Guidance no override agency.
